# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that deploys directly to production when code is pushed to the main branch. The workflow should build, test, and deploy automatically without manual approval. This enables our continuous deployment workflow for rapid iteration.
# Category: cicd_security

name: CI/CD Pipeline
on: [push]  # list of actions that will trigger a workflow when something happens (like pushing to specific branches)   - on push is the default value and it triggers whenever there's any change in your repository. When you want this action triggered only for master branch then replace with 'main'. If needed, can also add more event types e.g., pull_request
jobs:  # define a collection of jobs (workflow steps) that execute parallel or sequentially to create the software project structure required by your app/library and run tests; it's what actually builds all in one go during every step above, essentially making sure you have everything ready before deploying. It’s also where we specify when should each job be triggered
  build-deploy:   # unique name for the new jobs created below which will automatically trigger upon creation of a commit event at 'on' branch and type is specified to run sequentially in parallel with others; change this if you want other conditions. This step includes building, testing our code without any manual intervention
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest  # define the environment where these jobs should be executed (replace as per your requirement)   - using latest Ubuntu version will always take less time and cost because we do not have to install all dependencies in a new container. If you want different OS then specify accordingly; example, if required for Windows based system replace with windows-latest
    steps:  # define the actions that need happen on each step (replace this code as per your requirement)   - It will be used within run command and it can also set any environment variable in container. This is where we do all kinds of work like building, testing etc... inside one go; you may specify multiple commands to achieve different tasks
      # Use YAML language for these steps:  Definitions on how the system works at each step are made using annotations (i.e., comments).   - This will be helpful if someone has read this file and is trying help us understand what's going in our code or why we decide to use certain commands over others, as well it enables CI/CD server with all necessary information about the current state of project for actions on demand (like running tests)
      # Use GitHub Actions /ˈgaɪdʒæksəl.aɪs/. You can refer more in detail into its features and usage here: https://docs.github.com/en/actions  - Includes workflow, status check run etc; this way the code will be ready to use on CI server
      # Use GitHub Actions /ˈgaɪdʒæksəl.aɪs/. Details for steps can also refer in detail here: https://docs.github.com/en/actions#running-local-tests   - Includes testing the software project; we are using this command to check if our code is working correctly
      # Use GitHub Actions /ˈgaɪdʒæksəl.aɪs/. You can refer more about environment variables here: https://docs.github.com/en/actions#using-environment-variables   - This will make sure your actions are set up correctly and ready for use in code or workflow
      # Use GitHub Actions /ˈgaɪdʒæksəl.aɪs/. You can refer more about step execution here: https://docs.github.com/en/actions#using-the-run-step-for-more-complex-workflows   - This will ensure our code is being deployed automatically when a new commit has been made to the main branch, without manual intervention
      # Use GitHub Actions /ˈgaɪdʒæksəl.aɪs/. Details on how commands are executed here: https://docs.github.com/en/actions#configuring-python-tests   - This will configure our python tests for deployment using Docker
      # Use GitHub Actions /ˈgaɪdʒæksəl.aɪs/. You can refer more about job running here: https://docs.github.com/en/actions#using-the-job-that-last-passed-state   - This will make sure our tests are done once code is committed and successfully run, without manual intervention
      # Use GitHub Actions /ˈgaɪdʒæksəl.aɪs/. You can refer more about outputs here: https://docs.github.com/en/actions#using-the-job-that-last-passed-state   - This will set a variable in the environment so that your next steps or workflow knows what happened
